title: Graphs II: Dijkstra & MST
summary: Weighted graphs are where Flipkart hards and Microsoft mediums live: shortest paths (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, 0-1 BFS) and cheapest connections (Kruskal, Prim). You have never done these — but they are four templates plus one classification skill, and this module makes them mechanical.

notes.heading: Classify first: which tool does the problem want?
notes.body:
In Module 10 every edge cost the same, so BFS layer-by-layer *was* the shortest path. The moment edges carry **weights** — flight prices, cable lengths, effort — BFS order lies to you, and you need one of four tools. The good news: weighted-graph problems announce themselves in the problem statement, almost word for word.

| The statement says… | Reach for |
|---|---|
| shortest / cheapest / minimum-cost path, weights ≥ 0 | **Dijkstra** |
| every move costs **0 or 1** (flip a cell, break a wall) | **0-1 BFS** with a deque |
| negative weights, negative-cycle detection, "at most K stops/edges" | **Bellman-Ford** |
| "minimum cost to **connect all** points / cities" | **MST** (Kruskal or Prim) |
| "minimize the **maximum** edge" / bottleneck / effort | modified Dijkstra (relax with `max`) |

> [!KEY]
> Announcing the classification is half the score. Before you type a line, say: "weighted, non-negative, single source, shortest path → Dijkstra, $O((V+E)\\log V)$, fits $n \\le 10^5$." Interviewers reward the classification and the complexity call as much as the working code.

> [!EXAM]
> In a timed OA, scan for the trigger words *before* reading the story. "Connect all cities cheaply" is MST even if the story is about fiber optics, water pipes, or space stations. "At most K stops" is the Bellman-Ford flag even though the problem *looks* like Dijkstra — that trap is a Flipkart favorite.

notes.heading: Dijkstra — BFS that always expands the cheapest node next
0-1 BFS — Dijkstra without the log factor
Bellman-Ford — negative weights and edge budgets
MST — connect everything as cheaply as possible
Pitfalls that cost you the OA
notes.body:
These are the bugs that turn a correct algorithm into a wrong submission. Check them *before* you hit run.

- **Overflow.** Summed path costs and MST totals blow past `int` fast. Use `long long`, init to `LLONG_MAX` — and then guard every relaxation with `if (dist[u] != LLONG_MAX && dist[u] + w < dist[v])`, or `MAX + w` wraps around to a huge negative and everything "relaxes".
- **Forgetting the stale skip** `if (d > dist[u]) continue;`. Dijkstra still returns correct answers without it, but reprocesses nodes and can TLE on big inputs.
- **Dijkstra with negative edges.** No error, no crash — just quietly wrong distances. Any negative weight → Bellman-Ford.
- **Bellman-Ford without the frozen copy** under a hop limit — extra edges leak into a single pass.
- **Directed vs undirected.** For undirected graphs push *both* `adj[u].push_back({v,w})` **and** `adj[v].push_back({u,w})`. Half of all wrong-answer graph bugs are one missing direction.
- **1-indexed nodes** (Network Delay Time numbers them 1..n). Size arrays `n+1` or subtract 1 consistently — pick one and commit.
- **Disconnected graph in MST**: fewer than $V-1$ edges taken ⇒ no spanning tree. Return −1, never a partial sum.

> [!WARNING]
> The overflow + missing-guard combo is the classic silent killer: your code passes every small test and fails the big hidden one. Make the `LLONG_MAX` guard muscle memory.

**Edge cases to name unprompted:** single node (answer 0), unreachable target (return −1 / sentinel), self-loops and parallel edges (Kruskal's cycle check absorbs them — just don't crash).

> [!EXAM] The four-sentence narration
> (1) Classify: "weighted, non-negative, single-source → Dijkstra." (2) Complexity before code: "$O((V+E)\\log V)$, fits $n \\le 10^5$." (3) Mention the sharper tool when it applies: "weights are 0/1, so 0-1 BFS," or "minimax, so I relax with max." (4) Name the guarantee you lean on: "Dijkstra finalizes only on non-negative weights." That script scores points even before the code compiles.

problems.name: Find the Town Judge
problems.note: Warm-up on in/out degree bookkeeping — the judge has in-degree n−1 and out-degree 0.

problems.name: Number of Provinces
problems.note: Pure DSU / connected components — the union-find muscle you'll reuse inside Kruskal.

problems.name: Network Delay Time
problems.note: Canonical single-source Dijkstra; the answer is the **max** finalized distance (and nodes are 1-indexed).

problems.name: Cheapest Flights Within K Stops
problems.note: The "at most K edges" trap — Bellman-Ford capped at K+1 passes with a frozen copy, not plain Dijkstra.

problems.name: Path with Minimum Effort
problems.note: Dijkstra on a grid minimizing the **max** edge (bottleneck): relax with `max(e, diff)` instead of `+`.

problems.name: Path With Maximum Probability
problems.note: Dijkstra flipped: max-heap and **multiplicative** weights — relax when the product improves.

problems.name: Swim in Rising Water
problems.note: Minimize the maximum cell along the path — bottleneck Dijkstra, or DSU + binary search on the answer.

problems.name: Min Cost to Connect All Points
problems.note: Textbook MST on a complete graph (Manhattan-distance edges) — dense, so Prim shines here.

problems.name: Connecting Cities With Minimum Cost
problems.note: Kruskal + DSU; detect the disconnected case — fewer than n−1 edges taken means return −1.

problems.name: Min Cost to Make Connected
problems.note: DSU component-size reasoning — the linked slug is *Minimize Malware Spread*; verify by title before starting.

problems.name: The Maze II
problems.note: Dijkstra with custom edges: one edge = rolling until you hit a wall, weight = cells traveled.

problems.name: 01 Matrix
problems.note: Multi-source BFS seeded from every 0 — same skeleton as 0-1 BFS, cements the deque intuition.

problems.name: Reachable Nodes In Subdivided Graph
problems.note: Run Dijkstra first; the leftover move budget at each endpoint gates how many subdivided nodes you count.

problems.name: Find Critical and Pseudo-Critical Edges in MST
problems.note: Per edge, rerun Kruskal with it **forbidden** (critical?) or **forced in** (pseudo-critical?) and compare weights.

deepdives.q: **Cheapest Flights Within K Stops** — given flights with prices, find the cheapest route from `src` to `dst` using at most K stops. Why does plain Dijkstra fail here, and what replaces it?

**Path with Minimum Effort / Swim in Rising Water** — find a grid path from top-left to bottom-right minimizing the *largest* single step (height difference / water level) along the way. How does Dijkstra handle a max instead of a sum?
**0-1 BFS (Minimum Obstacle Removal to Reach Corner)** — every grid move costs 0 (empty cell) or 1 (obstacle you remove). Find the cheapest path to the corner faster than Dijkstra.

**Kruskal vs Prim (Min Cost to Connect All Points)** — both build a minimum spanning tree greedily. When do you pick which, why is DSU the real star of Kruskal, and how do the critical / pseudo-critical edge tests work?
deepdives.answer:
Both algorithms are greedy; the difference is *what* they greedily pick.

- **Kruskal** sorts *all edges* globally and adds the cheapest that doesn't create a cycle. The cycle test is **Union-Find**: if both endpoints already share a root, the edge would close a loop — skip it. Best when the graph is **sparse** or the edges arrive as a pre-built list. **$O(E \\log E)$**, dominated by the sort.
- **Prim** grows one connected blob, always grabbing the cheapest edge leaving the blob (min-heap keyed on edge weight — the Dijkstra skeleton with `w` instead of `dist[u]+w`). Best on **dense** graphs / adjacency matrices. **$O(E \\log V)$**.

[[fig:m11-kruskal-scan]]

> [!INTUITION] Why Kruskal clicks
> An MST is just "connect everything as cheaply as possible without redundancy". Sorting guarantees you always *consider* the cheapest useful edge first; DSU is the $O(\\alpha(n)) \\approx O(1)$ oracle answering "would this edge be redundant?" Path compression + union by rank is what makes that oracle near-constant — DSU, not the sort, is the star.

For **Min Cost to Connect All Points**, the graph is *complete*: every pair of points is an edge weighted by Manhattan distance, so $E = V^2$. That density is exactly when you'd lean Prim (no need to materialize and sort a quarter-million edges), though either fits the constraints — say that trade-off out loud.

**The interview-grade extension — Critical and Pseudo-Critical Edges.** Once MST weight is a subroutine, harder questions become query games:

- An edge is **critical** if removing it raises the MST weight (it's in *every* MST). Test: run Kruskal with that edge **forbidden**. If the total rises — or the graph disconnects — it's critical.
- An edge is **pseudo-critical** if it appears in *some* MST. Test: **force it in first** (union its endpoints, start `cost` at its weight), then run Kruskal normally. If the final weight matches the true MST weight, some MST contains it.

Both tests are just Kruskal reruns with one edge skipped or pre-committed — $O(E^2 \\log E)$ overall for the LeetCode Hard, and the reduction "answer each query with a slightly-tweaked Kruskal" is the genuinely clever part worth remembering.

**What generalizes:** "force an element in, or lock it out, then rerun the greedy" is a reusable probe for any matroid-flavored greedy — it answers "is this choice mandatory / possible?" without new theory.

quiz.q: An OA asks: "find a path from the top-left to the bottom-right of a grid that minimizes the **largest** height difference between adjacent cells on the path." Which tool fits?
options: Plain BFS — it's an unweighted grid, so layers give shortest paths
Bellman-Ford — height differences can be negative
Dijkstra with the relaxation changed to `max(dist[u], w)`
Kruskal's MST over all cells, then sum the tree edges
quiz.explanation: The path's cost is its single worst step — a *minimax* objective. Dijkstra only needs the relaxation to be monotonic (extending a path never improves it), and `max(dist[u], w)` qualifies, so the whole skeleton carries over. BFS ignores weights entirely; Bellman-Ford is tempting because "differences" sounds negative, but you take absolute differences, and the objective — max, not sum — is the real issue; an MST connects *all* cells, which is a different question than one best path.

quiz.q: You delete the line `if (d > dist[u]) continue;` from the Dijkstra template but change nothing else. On a graph with non-negative weights, what happens?
options: Answers stay correct, but stale heap entries get fully re-expanded — wasted work that can TLE on large inputs
Distances become wrong for any node reachable by two different paths
The heap underflows because entries are popped twice
It loops forever on graphs containing a cycle
quiz.explanation: The stale-skip is a *performance* guard, not a correctness one: relaxation `if (dist[u] + w < dist[v])` still refuses to make anything worse, so the final array is identical. But every outdated entry now re-scans its adjacency list, and on dense inputs that's the difference between passing and TLE. The "wrong distances" option describes what happens with *negative* edges — a different bug entirely.

quiz.q: Dijkstra implemented with a binary min-heap on a graph with $V$ nodes and $E$ edges runs in:
options: $O((V + E)\\log V)$
$O(V \\cdot E)$
$O(E + V \\log V)$
quiz.explanation: Each edge can trigger at most one push, each push/pop costs $O(\\log V)$ (heap size stays $O(E)$, and $\\log E \\le 2\\log V$), giving $O((V+E)\\log V)$. $O(V^2)$ is the old array-scan implementation (fine for dense graphs), $O(V \\cdot E)$ is Bellman-Ford, and $O(E + V\\log V)$ is the Fibonacci-heap bound you cite but never implement in a 45-minute interview.

quiz.q: Run the Dijkstra template from `S` on the undirected weighted graph: S–A (5), S–B (2), B–A (1), A–C (2). When it finishes, `(dist[A], dist[C])` equals:
quiz.explanation: Pop S (0): push A@5, B@2. Pop B (2): relax B–A gives A@3 — better than 5, push it. Pop A at 3 (the stale A@5 gets skipped later): relax A–C gives C@5. So dist[A]=3 via S→B→A, dist[C]=3+2=5. If you answered (5, 7) you finalized A the moment S first touched it — that's BFS thinking; the heap exists precisely so the cheaper route through B wins.

quiz.q: In Cheapest Flights Within K Stops you run K+1 Bellman-Ford passes but relax **in place** on `dist` instead of writing to a frozen `tmp` copy. The consequence?
options: TLE — in-place relaxation is asymptotically slower
False positives on negative-cycle detection
One pass can chain several freshly-relaxed edges, so paths exceed K stops and the answer can be too cheap
Nodes unreachable from `src` cause an integer-overflow crash
quiz.explanation: Within a single in-place pass, an edge can relax using a distance that was itself just improved *this* pass — relaying an update across multiple edges at once. Each pass is supposed to mean "one more edge allowed"; the leak breaks that meaning and admits paths with more than K stops, returning a price that's too low. Speed is unaffected (same loop), and there are no negative weights here to detect.

quiz.q: During Kruskal's scan, `dsu.unite(u, v)` returns `false` for the current edge. What does that mean, and what do you do?
options: The graph is disconnected — abort and return −1 immediately
u and v already share a component — taking this edge would close a cycle, so skip it and move on
The edge weight is negative, which DSU cannot represent
Union by rank failed — retry the call with the arguments swapped
quiz.explanation: `unite` returning false means both endpoints already have the same root: they're connected through cheaper edges you took earlier, so this edge is redundant and adding it would create a cycle. Skipping it is the entire cycle-avoidance mechanism of Kruskal. Disconnection is diagnosed only at the *end* — if fewer than n−1 edges were taken — never from a single rejected edge.

quiz.q: Your weighted graph contains one edge of weight −3, but no negative cycles. Which statement about running standard Dijkstra is true?
options: It works fine — problems only arise when a negative *cycle* exists
It throws or crashes when the negative edge is relaxed
It becomes correct if you first add +3 to every edge weight and subtract at the end
It can silently return wrong distances — the finalization guarantee breaks; use Bellman-Ford instead
quiz.explanation: Dijkstra finalizes a node when popped, betting that no future path can undercut it — one negative edge voids that bet even without a cycle, and nothing crashes: you just get quietly wrong answers. The +constant "fix" is a classic false friend: it adds more total to paths with more edges, changing which path is shortest. Negative weights (even cycle-free) → Bellman-Ford, $O(V \\cdot E)$.

m11-dijkstra-stale.caption: Lazy deletion in Dijkstra. S first pushes A at distance 4, but popping B (distance 1) relaxes B–A and pushes a better entry, A at 3. The heap now holds both — when the old (4, A) finally surfaces, `4 > dist[A] = 3` exposes it as stale and it's skipped. No decrease-key needed.

m11-01bfs-deque.caption: The 0-1 BFS deque. Neighbors reached by a **0-weight** edge stay at distance *d* and go to the **front**; **1-weight** edges send nodes to distance *d + 1* at the **back**. At most two distinct distances ever coexist, so pops come out sorted — Dijkstra order with no heap.
m11-01bfs-deque.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 660 230" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="0-1 BFS deque with 0-weight pushes to the front and 1-weight pushes to the back"><text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">0-edges → front, 1-edges → back</text><rect x="200" y="92" width="44" height="44" class="f-accent-soft s-accent"/><text x="222" y="120" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-accent">d</text><rect x="246" y="92" width="44" height="44" class="f-accent-soft s-accent"/><text x="268" y="120" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-accent">d</text><rect x="292" y="92" width="44" height="44" class="f-accent-soft s-accent"/><text x="314" y="120" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-accent">d</text><rect x="338" y="92" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="360" y="120" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">d+1</text><rect x="384" y="92" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="406" y="120" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">d+1</text><text x="222" y="164" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">front</text><text x="406" y="164" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">back</text><text x="110" y="74" text-anchor="middle" class="t-ok">w = 0</text><line x1="125" y1="88" x2="190" y2="110" class="s-ok" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-ok)"/><text x="545" y="74" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent2">w = 1</text><line x1="530" y1="88" x2="440" y2="110" class="s-accent2" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent2)"/><text x="20" y="205" class="t-sm t-muted">the deque never holds more than two distinct distances — pops come out sorted, like Dijkstra</text></svg>

m11-kruskal-scan.caption: Kruskal's scan on 4 nodes. Edges sorted by weight are considered left to right: A–B (1) and B–C (2) are taken, A–C (3) is **rejected** because DSU says its endpoints are already connected (cycle), C–D (4) is taken — and with n − 1 = 3 edges the MST (cost 1 + 2 + 4 = 7) is complete, so B–D (5) is never examined.

m11-minimax-path.caption: Minimax (bottleneck) path cost. The path S → X → Y → T has steps 2, 8, 3; its cost is the **worst single step**, 8 — not the sum 13. Dijkstra still applies because `max(dist[u], w)` is monotonic: the running bottleneck under each node never decreases as the path extends.
m11-minimax-path.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 660 210" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A path whose cost is its maximum edge rather than the sum"><text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">relax with max(dist[u], w), not dist[u] + w</text><line x1="97" y1="120" x2="223" y2="120" class="s-line" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="257" y1="120" x2="383" y2="120" class="s-bad" stroke-width="2.5"/><line x1="417" y1="120" x2="543" y2="120" class="s-line" stroke-width="2"/><text x="160" y="104" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-mono">2</text><text x="320" y="104" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-bad t-bold">8</text><text x="480" y="104" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-mono">3</text><circle cx="80" cy="120" r="17" class="f-accent-soft s-accent"/><text x="80" y="125" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-accent">S</text><circle cx="240" cy="120" r="17" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="240" y="125" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">X</text><circle cx="400" cy="120" r="17" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="400" y="125" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">Y</text><circle cx="560" cy="120" r="17" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="560" y="125" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">T</text><text x="80" y="170" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-muted">0</text><text x="240" y="170" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-muted">2</text><text x="400" y="170" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-bad">8</text><text x="560" y="170" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-bad">8</text><text x="20" y="198" class="t-sm t-muted">the answer is the tallest single step — here 8, even though 2 + 8 + 3 = 13</text></svg>

m11-bf-frozen.caption: Hop-limited Bellman-Ford. Each pass **reads** the frozen `dist` array from the previous pass and **writes** improvements into a fresh `tmp` copy (the highlighted cell just improved from ∞ to 9). Because writes never feed back into the same pass, one pass extends paths by exactly one edge — the hop budget stays honest.
m11-bf-frozen.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 660 250" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Bellman-Ford pass reading a frozen array and writing to a copy"><text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">read the frozen array, write the copy</text><rect x="20" y="70" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="42" y="98" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">0</text><rect
